title: A Rayleigh criterion for mechanical instability: inducing activity by chemo-mechanical coupling
authors: Aaron Beyen, Francesco Casini, Christian Maes
abstract: Instabilities in thermodynamic systems are often undesirable, as they can lead to loss of control or even catastrophic behavior. Yet, the same mechanisms can also generate rich nonequilibrium behavior and may play a constructive role in living systems. We introduce a theoretical framework, inspired by Rayleigh's analysis of thermoacoustic instabilities, to study the emergence of mechanical activity. In particular, we derive Rayleigh-like criteria governing the onset of activity and the generation of rotational motion in a slow Newtonian probe coupled to driven chemical processes, described by Markov jump processes. These criteria are expressed in terms of the phase relation between entropic and frenetic contributions, providing a transparent condition for when chemical driving results in sustained rotational or active mechanical motion.
summary: This paper provides a theoretical framework to understand how chemical energy can be converted into mechanical motion through feedback. It identifies a specific phase-relation criterion, analogous to Rayleigh's criterion for acoustic instability, that predicts when chemical driving leads to negative friction and sustained active motion.
main_problem: Determining the conditions under which non-equilibrium chemical driving generates mechanical instability, such as negative friction and sustained rotational motion, through chemo-mechanical coupling.
main_result: The authors derive a Rayleigh-like criterion for the onset of activity based on the phase relation between entropic and frenetic contributions, showing that negative friction can emerge when the frenetic (time-symmetric) component dominates.
method: The study uses a reduction/integration approach (Born-Oppenheimer-like) to integrate out fast chemical degrees of freedom, applying non-equilibrium linear response theory and singular perturbation theory in the weak coupling limit.
model_or_system: A Newtonian mechanical probe (underdamped particle) coupled to a chemical bath of $N$ independent Markov jump processes. The coupling is mediated by a potential $U(x, \sigma)$ that influences both the mechanical force and the chemical transition rates.
key_observables: Effective friction coefficient (specifically the frenetic and entropic parts), induced mean force (rotational/streaming term), noise amplitude, and stationary velocity/position distributions.
important_parameters: Chemical driving strength (affinity $\Delta\mu$ or $w$), coupling constant $\lambda$, phase angle $\phi$ between potential and reactivity, and the large driving limit $w 	o \infty$.
main_assumptions: Time-scale separation between fast chemical and slow mechanical dynamics, weak coupling limit ($\lambda \ll 1$), and the assumption of semi-reciprocity and local detailed balance.
figures_summary: Plots of the friction coefficient as a function of position and driving strength; velocity distributions showing transitions from Maxwellian to bimodal/asymmetric; and position distributions showing U-shaped profiles in the active regime.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of chemo-mechanical instability, defines the mathematical framework of the coupled system, derives the reduced Langevin dynamics for the probe, analyzes the onset of negative friction and rotational forces using a Rayleigh-like criterion, explores the large driving limit, and concludes with numerical simulations of the nonlinear/saturation regimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
